Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty loose this semester. I'd typically set aside chunks of time on weekends to binge-watch lecture videos and skim through materials, but during the week I was mostly just going through the motions. I have access to good resources and a decent setup at home, so I'd often study in my room with my tablet, making visual notes and diagrams. But I can't say I was super disciplined about it—I'd aim for consistency but often ended up cramming when things piled up.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what else was going on. I'm involved in extracurriculars, so some weeks I'd barely touch course materials until I realized a deadline was approaching. Other weeks, when things were calmer, I'd try to spread out my studying more evenly. But if I'm being honest, deadlines were usually what dictated when I actually sat down to focus on coursework rather than any real schedule I set for myself.
Q3: Usually it was either a notification about an upcoming deadline or just remembering that I hadn't checked in for a while. Sometimes I'd log in because I was bored and figured I should at least see what was happening in the course. It wasn't really habitual for me—more reactive than proactive. Occasionally I'd log in just to download materials so I could study them offline later without needing to be on the platform.
Q4: Not really, to be honest. The LMS never became something I naturally incorporated into my daily routine. There were brief periods where I'd check it more regularly, usually early in the semester when things felt fresh, but that faded pretty quickly. I think I just found the interface kind of uninspiring, and once I had the materials I needed, I didn't feel compelled to keep going back.
Q5: The video lectures were probably the most useful for me since I'm a visual learner. I could pause, rewind, and really absorb the diagrams and visual explanations at my own pace. I also appreciated when instructors posted slides or infographics—those were easier for me to study from than dense text documents. The discussion boards occasionally had helpful clarifications from other students, but I rarely participated myself.
Q6: I mostly ignored the discussion forums and peer interaction stuff. It felt like extra work that wasn't directly tied to my grade, and honestly, I just wasn't motivated enough to engage. The reading materials that were purely text-heavy I'd often skim or skip entirely because they didn't match how I learn best. If something wasn't visually engaging or clearly connected to an assignment, I probably didn't spend much time on it.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it was clearly going to help me on an assignment or exam, or if it presented information in a way that clicked for me visually. If I could see how something connected to what I'd be graded on, I'd engage with it. But if it felt like busywork or was too abstract, I'd lose interest quickly. I needed to see the point pretty immediately, or my attention would drift.
Q8: Yeah, optional activities felt completely different. Required ones I'd do even if I was bored because I knew they affected my grade. Optional stuff? I'd look at it and think, 'I could do this, or I could do literally anything else.' With my motivation being pretty low this semester, optional activities almost never made the cut. They felt like suggestions rather than priorities, and I had enough on my plate already.
Q9: Definitely. Once I downloaded lecture slides or videos, I'd study from those files on my tablet without being connected to the LMS at all. I'd make handwritten visual notes, create my own diagrams, and sometimes look up YouTube videos that explained concepts better than the course materials. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the platform, using my own methods that work better for how my brain processes information.
Q10: There were plenty of times I'd spend hours going through my downloaded notes and creating study guides, but none of that showed up anywhere in the system. I'd also study with a friend sometimes, quizzing each other or explaining concepts out loud, which obviously doesn't register online. My LMS activity probably looked way more minimal than the actual time I put into understanding the material.
Q11: All the time. I'd watch a video once on the LMS, download it or screenshot key frames, and then review those materials multiple times offline. Same with slides—I'd access them once to download, then study from the files repeatedly. The LMS would only show that single access, but I might have reviewed that content five or six times on my own device before an exam.
Q12: Around midterms, everything just felt harder. I had a lot going on with my extracurriculars, and the course material was getting more complex. I started feeling pretty bored with the content, which made it tough to push through. There were weeks where I'd open the LMS, stare at it, and just feel this wave of 'I don't want to do this' wash over me. My anxiety would spike a bit, but mostly I just felt disengaged.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was tired or overwhelmed with other responsibilities, the LMS was the first thing I'd neglect. I'd tell myself I'd catch up later, but 'later' kept getting pushed back. Competing responsibilities from my extracurriculars definitely ate into my study time. When I was confused about something, I'd sometimes avoid the materials altogether rather than face the frustration of not understanding.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd scroll through materials or have a video playing but my mind was completely elsewhere. I'd technically be 'engaged' according to the system, but I wasn't absorbing anything. It was like going through the motions—checking a box mentally without actually learning. Those sessions felt pretty pointless in retrospect, but at the time I convinced myself I was being productive.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew they were important. Part of it was just low motivation—the content didn't excite me, so I'd find reasons to delay. I'd tell myself I work better under pressure, which is partially true but also just an excuse. The anxiety of starting something felt worse than the anxiety of having it hang over me, weirdly enough. I'd avoid things until avoiding them became more stressful than just doing them.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to actually submit work. I'd have this pattern where I'd ignore something for weeks, then scramble in the last day or two to get it done. My LMS activity probably spiked dramatically right before due dates. Without deadlines, I honestly don't think I would have completed much at all this semester. They were stressful but necessary for someone with my motivation levels.
Q17: Feedback had a mixed effect on me. When I got decent feedback, it was mildly encouraging but didn't really change my behavior much. When feedback was critical, I'd feel a bit deflated and sometimes avoid that type of assignment for a while. I don't think feedback fundamentally altered how I engaged—I was pretty set in my patterns regardless. It was more of a momentary reaction than something that shaped my approach.
Q18: The course structure felt pretty standard, which meant it didn't really push me to engage more than I naturally would. Weekly modules were predictable, which was fine but not exciting. I think if there had been more variety or interactive elements, I might have been more engaged. The structure allowed me to coast at a minimal level, which I took full advantage of given my low motivation this semester.
Q19: The heavy reliance on text-based readings made things harder for me as a visual learner. When there were good diagrams or video explanations, engagement was easier. The discussion requirements felt forced and didn't add much value for me personally. Having materials available for download was great because it let me study in ways that worked better for me. But overall, the design didn't particularly accommodate different learning styles.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is searching for something specific or trying to understand materials but struggling. They might watch videos multiple times, click through different resources, looking for clarity but never finding it enough to complete the assignment confidently. Or maybe they're just procrastinating—opening things, getting distracted, coming back, never actually finishing. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity.
Q21: That actually sounds a lot like me. I didn't engage much with the LMS itself, but I studied offline quite a bit and managed to pass. I think if you understand how you learn best and have access to good resources outside the platform, you can perform okay without high LMS activity. The system doesn't capture everything—it misses all the offline studying, the external resources, the thinking you do away from the screen.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you're active, how often you log in, what you click on. But they miss the quality of that engagement entirely. They can't tell if you're actually learning or just scrolling mindlessly. They miss offline studying, external resources, discussions with classmates in person. They show behavior but not understanding, activity but not actual engagement with the material.
Q23: To me, being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and wanting to understand it, not just complete it. It's when you're curious, asking questions, making connections. This semester, I wasn't really engaged by that definition—I was just getting through it. Real engagement feels different from just doing the minimum to pass. It's about being mentally present and invested, which I struggled with.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I actually studied offline using downloaded materials and my own visual notes. It wouldn't capture my emotional state—the boredom, the low motivation, the mild anxiety that made me avoid things. It wouldn't show the CBT session I did to try to manage my stress, or how my extracurriculars competed for my attention. The data shows what I did on the platform, but not why I did it or what was happening in my life around it.
